title: Three Analogies: Friends with the Boss, Bride of the King, Children of the Father
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 09-24-23 -PM- Three Analogies - John 15-13-15 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Completing the Series on Joy in Serving Christ
subpoints: Part 4 (final) of a series on the joy of serving Christ
Three descriptions/analogies to help regain or keep joy in serving the Lord

outline.title: Analogy 1: Friends with the Boss (John 15:11-15)
subpoints: Jesus does not call his disciples merely servants but friends
He defines a friend as someone he would die for — and has died for
This is not an empty nicety (like an "I appreciate you" bumper sticker) — it is demonstrated friendship
If we view Jesus as a distant boss, we serve to impress him or those with his ear (middle management)
If we rightly think of Jesus as a friend who died for us, the motive to impress the boss disappears
Jesus tells us why he does things a certain way — he explains his commands as a friend, not merely orders as a boss
John 15:15: 'I have called you friends, for all things that I heard from my Father I have made known to you'
Working for a friend produces more dedication, not less; we serve because we want to, not because we have to

outline.title: Analogy 2: Bride of the King (Ephesians 5:22-27)
subpoints: The Cinderella story is a pale reflection of the truest story: Prince Jesus left his palace to find a slave and make her his bride
Jesus loves his bride sacrificially (Eph 5:25): 'Christ also loved the church and gave himself for her'
Not a romantic gesture — he died to save her; secured her perfection and took upon himself the guilt of her sins
Jesus loves his undeserving bride: not the beautiful Cinderella, but the unfaithful Gomer (Hosea 3)
Hosea bought Gomer back — the cheated husband walked into her pimp's house and paid with his own money
We are Gomer: the bride Jesus sacrificed himself for is a prostitute who turns to other lovers
Jesus loves his bride enough to change her (Eph 5:26-27): sanctifying and cleansing her to present her glorious
Kate Middleton became Duchess simply by whom she married; we walk on the arm of the King
We serve him as a wife who serves a husband who loves her sacrificially

outline.title: Analogy 3: Children of the Father (Luke 15:11-32; Romans 8:15-17)
subpoints: God does not need our service — the sustainer of the universe is not powerless without us
He has us work with him because of the relationship we have with him as his children
Like a father who lets his young child help with a project — not because of the help, but because he is his child
The prodigal son wrote a speech to offer himself as a servant — but the father embraced him as a son
The son was right that he did not deserve to be called his father's son; but the son does not get to decide — that is solely the father's call

Romans 8:15-17: 'You received the Spirit of adoption by whom you cry out, Abba, Father. The Spirit himself bears witness with our spirit that we are children of God, and if children then heirs — heirs of God and joint heirs with Christ'
The treasure of Christianity is not that we get heaven, blessings, hope, peace, or joy — it is that we get God, as our Father
We are not merely servants, not simply friends — we are his children

outline.title: Conclusion
subpoints: Thinking through these three analogies will allow us to serve without sinking
We will have joy instead of being burdened by serving Christ in his church
We are friends with the Boss; we are the Bride of the King; we are the Children of the Father

summary:
Pastor Tito Lyro delivers the fourth and final installment of a series on joy in serving Christ, presenting three analogies drawn from Scripture to help Christians regain or maintain joy in Christian service. The sermon text is John 15:11-15, in which Jesus declares that he no longer calls his disciples servants but friends, defining friendship by its ultimate expression: laying down one's life.

The first analogy — friends with the boss — addresses the relational distance many believers feel in their service. When Jesus is viewed as a distant employer, service becomes an attempt to impress him or those close to him. But Jesus demonstrated his friendship by dying for us, and he explains his commands to us as a friend explains his reasoning — not merely issuing orders. Working for a friend produces greater dedication and joy, not less. The second analogy — bride of the king — draws on Ephesians 5:22-27 and the story of Hosea and Gomer. Jesus is not Prince Charming who marries the beautiful Cinderella; he is the husband who seeks out his unfaithful, prostituted wife, walks into her captivity, and buys her back with his own blood. We are Gomer. And he loves his undeserving bride enough to sanctify and cleanse her, presenting her glorious without spot or wrinkle. This transforms service into the glad response of a wife who serves the husband who loves her sacrificially.

The third analogy — children of the Father — addresses the mistaken belief that God needs our service. The parable of the prodigal son illustrates the point: the son prepared a speech to offer himself as a hired servant, but the father embraced him as a son before he finished speaking. The son was right that he did not deserve to be called his father's son — but that is solely the father's call. Romans 8:15-17 declares that we have received the Spirit of adoption by whom we cry "Abba, Father." The ultimate treasure of Christianity is not blessings, hope, or peace — it is that we get God himself, as our Father.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: John 15:11-15

I am going to move over here because I am going to project some things. We are finishing today our four-part mini-series on the joy of serving Christ. This afternoon I want to share three different pictures — or analogies — that might help us have joy in serving Christ.

I am going to start by reading John 15, starting at verse 11. This is the Word of our Lord.

"These things I have spoken to you, that my joy may remain in you, and that your joy may be full. This is my commandment, that you love one another as I have loved you. Greater love has no one than this, than to lay down one's life for his friends. You are my friends if you do whatever I command you. No longer do I call you servants, for a servant does not know what his master is doing; but I have called you friends, for all things that I heard from my Father I have made known to you. You did not choose me, but I chose you and appointed you that you should go and bear fruit, and that your fruit should remain, that whatever you ask the Father in my name he may give you. These things I command you, that you love one another."

Let us pray. Father, we pray that you would open our eyes to see great things concerning you in this passage and others. We pray that you would help us to stay attentive to your word. We pray in Jesus's name. Amen.

All right. If we have been in church long enough, we have sung — or at least heard the children's song — "I've Got the Joy." Right? "I've got the joy, joy, joy, joy — down in my heart. Where? Down in my heart..." I am not going to sing anymore to you. Yet when it comes to serving the Lord, all this talk of joy and happiness can often be just that — talk, and not a reality. We often lose joy as we relate to people in the church, and as we try to do the things that need to be done at church and in Christian service in general.

I think this is in great part due to wrong thinking on our part. Last Sunday morning and this morning I have been trying to re-configure the way that we think about serving in relationship to Christ. So let me emphasize this afternoon three descriptions — three illustrations or analogies — that I think will help us regain or keep our joy in serving the Lord.

The three descriptions are: we are **friends with the Boss**; we are the **Bride of the King**; and we are **children of the Father**.

## Analogy 1: Friends with the Boss

In John 15:13-15, Jesus tells us that he no longer calls us servants but calls us friends. And he defines a friend of his as someone he would die for — and has died for. When Jesus says that his disciples are not merely servants but friends, this is not just an empty line to make us feel good. He is not Facebook friends with us — like the bumper sticker that says "I appreciate you," which means absolutely nothing because the driver does not even know who is behind him. When Jesus says "I am your friend," he has demonstrated that friendship by dying for us. It is not an empty nicety.

This declaration helps us reshape our motives for serving Jesus. If we have a view of Jesus as being our fairly distant boss, then we may try to serve him out of a motivation to impress him — or if not him, at least middle management: the church leaders and those who we perceive have the ear of the boss. But if we rightly think of Jesus as a friend who was willing to give his life for us, then the motivation to do things in order to impress the boss will not make any sense. We will be free to enjoy serving him, and free to enjoy serving the other friends for whom Jesus died.

Jesus as our friend also tells us why he wants things done a certain way. He says, "I am your friend, because — for all things that I heard from my Father, I have made known to you" (John 15:15). He goes beyond telling us that he is our boss; he tells us he is our friend, and he tells us why. He reviews to us our Father. None of this is to say that we are not servants — but we are not serving some giant corporation with layers of management and a boss who is so busy and important you never see him and can never know him. We are working for our friend.

And the problem with that imagery is that sometimes we tend to think that if we are working for our friend, we do not have to work as hard. That is not biblical thinking — if we work for those we care about, we are more dedicated, not less. When we serve as Jesus's friend, we find that our serving changes: we do not serve because we have to or need to, but because we want to. After all, we are friends. And the joy returns.

## Analogy 2: Bride of the King

Would you turn to Ephesians 5 for a moment? Starting at verse 22. We are the bride of the King, and that should help us in having joy in serving God.

One of the most popular fairy tales is Cinderella. John Hendley, writing about this idea, describes it this way: when a girl is scrubbing floors and scouring pots, then a horse winds up outside and the door is blocked by a tall, handsome man holding a perfectly fitting glass slipper. The next day, Princess Cinderella wakes up in silk, eating next to her new husband. And then he comments: "Weddings are easier to organize in fairy tales."

But he continues: this plot line is not confined to fairy tales. It is the deepest truth that the cosmos knows. Prince Charming is a pale reflection of the Prince who left his lofty palace to find a slave and make her into his bride. The truth is far more beautiful than the make-believe because the Husband is far more wonderful. And we see that this is the Lord Jesus Christ.

Jesus loves his bride sacrificially. Prince Charming gave up his reputation to marry Cinderella — he gave up any chance of a strategic marriage. But in the history of redemption, Jesus Christ gave up more than that. Ephesians 5:25: "Husbands, love your wives, just as Christ also loved the church and gave himself for her." Jesus Christ gave up his life for his bride. The greatest act of love in history was when the church's husband died on a cross. He wasn't making a silly romantic gesture — he died to save her. Verse 26: "That he might sanctify and cleanse her with the washing of water by the word." In his life and death, Jesus did two things for us: he secured our perfection and holiness, and he took upon himself the guilt of our sins.

But Jesus loves his undeserving bride. Imagine if Prince Charming did something even stranger than marrying the lovely Cinderella. Imagine if he came and married the ugliest of the two ugly sisters — knowing exactly what she was like — and kept on loving her and being charming to her even as she cheated on him with equally ugly men. Imagine if he did all that. What I just described is not a fairy tale. It is the history of Hosea in the Bible.

God tells Hosea to marry an unfaithful wife, Gomer. When he does, she responds by continuing to sleep around, even having children with other men. She ends up selling herself into sex slavery. And yet, in Hosea 3:1-2, God says: "Then the Lord said to me, 'Go again, love a woman who is loved by a lover and is committing adultery, just like the love of the Lord for the children of Israel.'" So Hosea does: "So I bought her for myself for fifteen shekels of silver and one and one-half homers of barley." Hosea walks into her pimp's house and pays with his own money to buy her back.

Brothers and sisters — we are Gomer. Not the victim Cinderella — we are Gomer. The bride Jesus sacrificed himself for is a prostitute. This wife, the church, has the greatest of all husbands, but she turns away from him and runs after other lovers. And instead of turning his back on us, he comes for us. Jesus had to pay the price of his own blood to buy us out of slavery.

Jesus loves his bride enough to change her. Kate Middleton walked into Westminster Abbey simply as Miss Middleton, and she left as a Duchess. Her status was totally changed just because of whom she married. And the same has happened to us. The people of God are not mere servants — we have been given a beautiful white dress and we walk on the arm of the King. And as we walk with the King, he begins to transform us. Ephesians 5:26-27: "That he might sanctify and cleanse her with the washing of water by the word, that he might present her to himself a glorious church, not having spot or wrinkle or any such thing, but that she should be holy and without blemish." Jesus marries us as we are, but he loves us too much to leave us that way.

Brothers and sisters, we are not serving Christ as paid servants. We are serving him as a wife who serves the husband who loves her. And that should help us in gaining or keeping joy in the service.

## Analogy 3: Children of the Father

Lots of people do lots of things in the service of God — but the sustainer of the universe is not powerless without them. God does not need our service. So why does he have us work with him? Because of the relationship we have with him as his children.

If you have kids, you have had those times when your child came to help you with a project. Maybe they were trying to help you paint, or drill, or do something around the house. And you go along with it — not because of the great help they are providing, but because that is your child right there. You enjoy being with him or her.

Brothers and sisters, we are undeserving children. In Luke 15, the prodigal son rehearses his speech: "Father, I have sinned against heaven and before you, and I am no longer worthy to be called your son. Make me like one of your hired servants." The speech is completely accurate and true. He acted as if his father were dead — demanded the inheritance, spent it all on riotous living. Now he just hopes there might be enough compassion in the father to take him back as a servant.

There is no way, however, that his father will have him back as a servant. The parable continues: "And he arose and came to his father. But when he was still a great way off, his father saw him and had compassion, and ran and fell on his neck and kissed him. And the son said to him, 'Father, I have sinned against heaven and in your sight, and am no longer worthy to be called your son.' But the father said to his servants, 'Bring out the best robe and put it on him, and put a ring on his hand and sandals on his feet. And bring the fatted calf here and kill it, and let us eat and be merry; for this my son was dead and is alive again; he was lost and is found.'" Before the son gets to the end of his prepared speech, the father embraces him and calls for everything that would mark him as a son: a robe, sandals, a ring, a feast.

Now we need to remember that the son was right in saying that he does not deserve to be called this man's son. He is right. The thing is — the son does not get to decide whether he is a son or not. It is solely the father's call. And by faith, brothers and sisters, God has declared us to be his children. Romans 8:15-17: "For you did not receive the spirit of bondage again to fear, but you received the Spirit of adoption by whom you cry out, 'Abba, Father.' The Spirit himself bears witness with our spirit that we are children of God, and if children, then heirs — heirs of God and joint heirs with Christ, if indeed we suffer with him, that we may also be glorified together."

This is the treasure of Christianity. Not that we get to be good. Not that we get heaven, not blessings, not joy, not hope, not peace. The treasure of Christianity is that we get God — and we get him as our Father. We get to be with God as he works, and we get to be part of how God works. The God on the throne of heaven is our Father. We are not merely servants. Not simply friends. We are his children.

## Conclusion

Think about these three analogies of our relationship to the Lord:
- We are **friends with the Boss**.
- We are the **Bride of the King**.
- We are the **Children of the Father**.

As we do that, they will allow us to serve him without sinking. We will be able to have joy instead of being burdened by serving Christ in his church.

## Closing Prayer

Father in heaven, thank you. You are so faithful in revealing yourself to us in all kinds of ways that we can relate. We thank you that Jesus is our friend. We thank you that he is our bridegroom and that you are our Father. We pray, Father, that you would enable us to serve you with joy in light of these realities. We pray in Jesus's name. Amen.
